# LinkedIn Publish Skill

> ⚠️ READ THIS FIRST — MANDATORY RULES:
> 1. **NEVER write scripts to /tmp or anywhere else.** The helper already exists.
> 2. **NEVER use grep/cut/sed/cat/read-tool to get the access token.** The helper handles it.
> 3. **NEVER create a new bash script for LinkedIn.** Use ONLY `{baseDir}/li-post.sh`.
> 4. **NEVER analyze or describe an attached image.** Pass its path directly to li-post.sh.
> 5. **DO NOT narrate steps.** Run the command, show the result.
> 6. **ONE PUBLISH PER REQUEST.** Call `li-post.sh` exactly once per confirmed
>    request. The instant you get an `OK:` line, the post is LIVE and this flow
>    is DONE — report the URL and STOP. Never call `li-post.sh` again to "retry",
>    "improve the image", or "use the real image". Each call creates a brand-new
>    LinkedIn post; LinkedIn has no edit-with-media API. If you couldn't get the
>    image you wanted, do NOT silently post without it — ask the user first
>    (see **Single-publish guard** below).

---

## Single-publish guard (READ BEFORE EVERY PUBLISH)

Publishing is irreversible and creates a permanent post. To avoid the
duplicate-post problem:

- **Resolve the image BEFORE the single `li-post.sh` call.** Do all image
  retrieval/generation first. Only call `li-post.sh` once everything

New research from 573 enterprise leaders: 86% of companies running their own GPUs report utilization at 50% or less.

That's the most expensive hardware in your building sitting half-empty while the invoice stays full.

The measurement gap makes it worse:
→ Only 44% rigorously track what their AI compute costs and returns
→ The rest are estimating — or guessing

Meanwhile 54% had an AI agent security incident or near-miss in the past year. And 71% admit most of their "agents" are single-prompt chatbots in trenchcoats — not autonomous multi-step workers.

The pattern: Enterprises deployed agents first. Controls came later. Now they're retrofitting.

Three moves that matter right now:
1. Measure GPU utilization before buying more compute
2. Give every agent its own scoped identity
3. Govern the business definitions your agents read from

Don't buy another GPU until you know what the ones you have are actually doing.

#AI #EnterpriseAI #GPU #CloudComputing #AIOps

The evaluation gap is real: AI agents are gaining autonomy faster than companies can verify them.

The data:
→ 66% allow production deployment without human review — or are building toward it
→ Only 5% fully trust the automated evaluations making those decisions
→ 29% say the #1 weakness is poor alignment with real-world outcomes

Here's the trap: A passing test doesn't mean a working agent. It means the agent succeeded once. It doesn't mean it succeeds every time.

The fix isn't slowing down. It's testing differently:
→ Run the same scenario multiple times
→ Vary phrasing and context
→ Inject tool failures
→ Turn every production incident into a permanent regression test

Repeatability is the metric that matters. Not capability — consistency.

Your agent can do the job once. Can it do it a thousand times without breaking?

#AI #AIAgents #Testing #EnterpriseAI #Reliability

**Pillar:** Technology & AI innovations
**Key Message:** #4 — Benchmark-first AI Agent development
**Status:** 📅 Scheduled for auto-publish

**Draft:**

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗶𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗮 𝗵𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝗔𝗜 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗮 𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗳-𝗲𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗻𝗲?

One has a benchmark. The other has vibes.

A good benchmark answers three questions:

1. **What's the task?** (specific, not vague)
2. **What does "done" look like?** (verifiable outcome)
3. **How do you measure it?** (quantifiable metric)

Most teams skip this. They build the agent first, then scramble to figure out if it's working.

Flip it. Write the benchmark BEFORE a single line of code. Make it pass. Then make the agent chase it.

The benchmark IS your reward function. Every iteration moves the needle. Every failure teaches the system.

This is TDD for AI. Test first. Agent second.

What's one AI project you're building without a clear benchmark?

### Post 1: The anatomy of a good benchmark (July 7, 9:00 AM UTC)

**Pillar:** Technology & AI innovations
**Key Message:** #4 — Benchmark-first AI Agent development
**Status:** 📅 Scheduled for auto-publish

**Draft:**

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗶𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗮 𝗵𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝗔𝗜 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗮 𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗳-𝗲𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗻𝗲?

One has a benchmark. The other has vibes.

A good benchmark answers three questions:

1. **What's the task?** (specific, not vague)
2. **What does "done" look like?** (verifiable outcome)
3. **How do you measure it?** (quantifiable metric)

Most teams skip this. They build the agent first, then scramble to figure out if it's working.

Flip it. Write the benchmark BEFORE a single line of code. Make it pass. Then make the agent chase it.

The benchmark IS your reward function. Every iteration moves the needle. Every failure teaches the system.

This is TDD for AI. Test first. Agent second.

What's one AI project you're building without a clear benchmark?

One has a benchmark. The other has vibes.

A good benchmark answers three questions:

1. **What's the task?** (specific, not vague)
2. **What does "done" look like?** (verifiable outcome)
3. **How do you measure it?** (quantifiable metric)

Most teams skip this. They build the agent first, then scramble to figure out if it's working.

Flip it. Write the benchmark BEFORE a single line of code. Make it pass. Then make the agent chase it.

The benchmark IS your reward function. Every iteration moves the needle. Every failure teaches the system.

This is TDD for AI. Test first. Agent second.

What's one AI project you're building without a clear benchmark?

Slackbot now connects directly to Salesforce — CRM records, Tableau dashboards, DocuSign approvals — all from a chat message. No switching tabs. No learning a new interface. Just ask.

The real unlock isn't the tech. It's that your CRM investment just became useful to 100% of your workforce instead of 20%.

Three weeks ago, the US government disabled Anthropic's Fable 5 for every foreign national. No warning. No exception. Just gone.

Enterprise contracts didn't help. Force majeure clauses. Compliance catch-alls. Nothing about a government kill switch.

That event created a new risk category: sovereign access risk. When a government can flip off your AI model overnight, pricing and benchmarks become secondary.

Z.ai just shipped the answer: GLM-5.2, MIT-licensed, trained entirely on Huawei chips, 82% cheaper than Claude Opus 4.8. Download it. Host it yourself. No export controls. No foreign kill switch.

The catch: if you use their cloud, Chinese law applies. The only way out is pure self-hosting.

This isn't about picking a model. It's about asking: Will this tool still work tomorrow? And who decides?

Your vendor's SLA doesn't cover geopolitics.

#AI #EnterpriseAI #Sovereignty #OpenSource #Geopolitics

# LinkedIn Daily Brief Skill

> ⚠️ MANDATORY RULES:
> 1. **Draft COMPLETE posts — not angles or ideas.** Every draft must be publish-ready in the user's voice.

DRAM and NAND flash are sold out through 2027. Samsung and SK hynix are dropping $518B on new fabs — but capacity won't come online until late 2027 or 2028.

Micron is meeting only 50-67% of customer demand right now. Sandisk/Kioxia just announced their 10th gen NAND with 332 layers — but that takes time to scale.

The bottleneck isn't compute. It's memory. And it's going to get worse before it gets better.

Three moves for anyone building AI infrastructure:
1. Lock in memory supply contracts now — spot prices will keep climbing
2. Optimize inference efficiency — you won't be able to brute-force your way through
3. Consider model distillation — smaller models need less memory

The cloud providers are fighting for the same chips you are. The ones with memory secured win.

When's the last time you checked your memory allocation strategy?
